tweets.text:
What is happening in Hong Kong?

Chinese semiconductor exports to Hong Kong rose to a record $15 billion in May.

This figure has soared over +200% since early 2025.

At the same time, Chinese chip exports to the world surged to a record $35 billion, tripling over the same period.

As a result, Hong Kong accounted for 43% of China’s semiconductor exports in May.

Over the first 5 months of 2026, China exported $239 billion of chips globally, with Hong Kong absorbing over 50% of this total, a record proportion and up from ~33% a decade ago.

Hong Kong has become a dominant logistics hub for China’s chip trade.
tweets.full_text:
What is happening in Hong Kong?

Chinese semiconductor exports to Hong Kong rose to a record $15 billion in May.

This figure has soared over +200% since early 2025.

At the same time, Chinese chip exports to the world surged to a record $35 billion, tripling over the same period.

As a result, Hong Kong accounted for 43% of China’s semiconductor exports in May.

Over the first 5 months of 2026, China exported $239 billion of chips globally, with Hong Kong absorbing over 50% of this total, a record proportion and up from ~33% a decade ago.

Hong Kong has become a dominant logistics hub for China’s chip trade.

tweets.text:
Full-time jobs in the US are falling at a concerning rate:

Full-time employment dropped -514,000 in June, to 133.66 million, the lowest since December 2024.

This marks the 3rd consecutive monthly decline, totaling -1.01 million. 

Since January 2025, full-time employment has now fallen -2.24 million

This also brings full-time employment back to levels seen in Q1 2023.

Meanwhile, the full-time employment-to-population ratio, which measures the proportion of the population working full-time, is down to 48.5%, from 50.5% in 2022, the lowest since mid-2021.

The weakness in the US labor market is accelerating.
tweets.full_text:
Full-time jobs in the US are falling at a concerning rate:

Full-time employment dropped -514,000 in June, to 133.66 million, the lowest since December 2024.

This marks the 3rd consecutive monthly decline, totaling -1.01 million. 

Since January 2025, full-time employment has now fallen -2.24 million

This also brings full-time employment back to levels seen in Q1 2023.

Meanwhile, the full-time employment-to-population ratio, which measures the proportion of the population working full-time, is down to 48.5%, from 50.5% in 2022, the lowest since mid-2021.

The weakness in the US labor market is accelerating.

tweets.text:
Japanese individual investors are buying the dip at a record pace:

Retail investors purchased +$5.9 billion of Japanese equities in the week ended June 26th, the largest weekly total on record.

At the same time, foreign funds sold -$7.7 billion, the largest weekly outflow since March.

This follows -$6.0 billion in sales by retail in the prior 2 weeks.

Stocks linked to AI and semiconductors were hit hardest last week, with SoftBank, Kioxia, and Furukawa Electric each falling at least -12% during the week.

Despite the pullback, the Nikkei 225 surged +37% in Q2 2026, its best quarterly performance on record, fueled by AI-related names.

Retail dip buying has become a global trend.

tweets.text:
💼 We keep hearing that AI is going to replace workers but the first serious data tells another story.

📊 Companies with high AI adoption saw their workforce increase by around +10.2% in the two years following adoption. The signal is even stronger for junior roles with around +12% growth in entry-level positions. At this stage, AI does not look only like a job-destruction machine but more like a growth accelerator for companies that know how to use it properly.

📈 AI automates tasks, speeds up execution, reduces costs and increases productivity but if that productivity allows a company to sell more, serve more customers, launch new products faster or gain market share, then it can actually end up hiring more people.

➡️ We often hear that AI will kill entry-level jobs but this suggests the opposite among companies that adopt AI aggressively because a junior employee equipped with AI can become productive much faster. AI lowers the entry barrier, accelerates training and allows less experienced profiles to produce more.

⚠️ However, I believe we need to stay cautious because companies investing the most in AI are often already the most dynamic, best capitalized and most growth-oriented but the core message is powerful which is that AI is going to redistribute value.

The winners will be the companies using it to scale, not just to cut costs.

*FT link: https://t.co/7yPxSAB19F

The signal is even stronger for junior roles with around +12% growth in entry-level positions. At this stage, AI does not look only like a job-destruction machine but more like a growth accelerator for companies that know how to use it properly.

📈 AI automates tasks, speeds up execution, reduces costs and increases productivity but if that productivity allows a company to sell more, serve more customers, launch new products faster or gain market share, then it can actually end up hiring more people.

➡️ We often hear that AI will kill entry-level jobs but this suggests the opposite among companies that adopt AI aggressively because a junior employee equipped with AI can become productive much faster. AI lowers the entry barrier, accelerates training and allows less experienced profiles to produce more.

⚠️ However, I believe we need to stay cautious because companies investing the most in AI are often already the most dynamic, best capitalized and most growth-oriented but the core message is powerful which is that AI is going to redistribute value.

The winners will be the companies using it to scale, not just to cut costs.

*FT link: https://t.co/7yPxSAB19F

tweets.text: On this day in 1776, the Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence, a document rooted in a decade-long fight over who had the right to tax whom. To finance the revolution it declared, Congress printed $241 million in Continental dollars between 1775 and 1779, backed by nothing but the promise of future tax revenue from states that didn't yet exist as a nation. By 1781, the currency had lost over 99% of its value, giving rise to the expression "not worth a Continental." The hyperinflation traumatized the Founders so deeply that they wrote a gold and silver clause into Article I of the Constitution, prohibiting states from making anything but precious metals legal tender. It took Alexander Hamilton's financial plan of 1790 to restructure the war debt and restore the country's ability to borrow.

To finance the revolution it declared, Congress printed $241 million in Continental dollars between 1775 and 1779, backed by nothing but the promise of future tax revenue from states that didn't yet exist as a nation. By 1781, the currency had lost over 99% of its value, giving rise to the expression "not worth a Continental." The hyperinflation traumatized the Founders so deeply that they wrote a gold and silver clause into Article I of the Constitution, prohibiting states from making anything but precious metals legal tender. It took Alexander Hamilton's financial plan of 1790 to restructure the war debt and restore the country's ability to borrow.

tweets.text:
🚨 The real warning signal for AI may not come from the tech giants but from their customers. 

➡️ For now, the infrastructure sellers continue to capture most of the value but the sectors expected to use AI heavily are not yet showing a spectacular acceleration in earnings. Normally, AI should allow companies to improve productivity, reduce costs, expand margins and ultimately grow profits. However, at this stage, we can clearly see the revenues of those selling the promise but much less the economic gains of those buying it.

⚠️ That does not mean AI is a bubble as productivity gains take time but the market will gradually start asking for proof. Not just announcements, speeches or AI partnerships, but visible results in the numbers with higher margins, stronger cash flow and faster earnings growth. If the promise remains concentrated among compute sellers, the market may eventually question whether the spending is truly justified.

This is not a crash signal but it is clearly a signal to stay vigilant.

*FT link: https://t.co/P4q3zjuzYA

🚨 The real warning signal for AI may not come from the tech giants but from their customers. 

➡️ For now, the infrastructure sellers continue to capture most of the value but the sectors expected to use AI heavily are not yet showing a spectacular acceleration in earnings. Normally, AI should allow companies to improve productivity, reduce costs, expand margins and ultimately grow profits. However, at this stage, we can clearly see the revenues of those selling the promise but much less the economic gains of those buying it.

⚠️ That does not mean AI is a bubble as productivity gains take time but the market will gradually start asking for proof. Not just announcements, speeches or AI partnerships, but visible results in the numbers with higher margins, stronger cash flow and faster earnings growth. If the promise remains concentrated among compute sellers, the market may eventually question whether the spending is truly justified.

This is not a crash signal but it is clearly a signal to stay vigilant.

*FT link: https://t.co/P4q3zjuzYA

tweets.text:
Happy 4th of July! 🇺🇸

America has given me and my family opportunities we could only have dreamed of, and some of the most meaningful moments of my life have happened here.

I’m deeply grateful for that.

I hope my comments on the economy are never taken as a criticism of this country. Quite the opposite.

I believe it’s important to understand where we are today so we can have thoughtful conversations about the future.

One of the defining macro themes of the next decade, in my view, will be whether America can restore the fiscal and monetary discipline that made it the world’s financial leader.

https://t.co/4i6xp6vine

tweets.text: @LanceRoberts Happy to show you that one. Same trend. No changes on conclusions in my view

thread_tweets.text: @LanceRoberts Happy to show you that one. Same trend. No changes on conclusions in my view
thread_tweets.full_text: @LanceRoberts Happy to show you that one. Same trend. No changes on conclusions in my view

thread_tweets.text:
There is one important difference between your chart and mine.

The data I’m using measures foreign holdings relative to total outstanding Treasury securities, excluding intragovernmental holdings.

What it shows is that the surge in Treasury issuance is no longer being absorbed by foreign buyers. 

That doesn’t necessarily mean foreigners are net sellers as a group — it simply means they are no longer keeping pace with the growth in supply.
thread_tweets.full_text:
There is one important difference between your chart and mine.

The data I’m using measures foreign holdings relative to total outstanding Treasury securities, excluding intragovernmental holdings.

What it shows is that the surge in Treasury issuance is no longer being absorbed by foreign buyers. 

That doesn’t necessarily mean foreigners are net sellers as a group — it simply means they are no longer keeping pace with the growth in supply.

thread_tweets.text:
It’s foreign official holdings (central banks + SWFs, ~$3.9T), not total foreign. 

And it’s market value from TIC, so part of the post-2021 drop is rate-driven price decline, not just selling. 

Par-adjusted would be cleaner but TIC doesn’t make that easy.

And yes — market value, so the 2022 rate spike exaggerates the decline vs. actual quantity sold. 

So, mostly issuance outgrowing a flat official bid, with the 2022 rate shock adding a valuation kicker.
thread_tweets.full_text:
It’s foreign official holdings (central banks + SWFs, ~$3.9T), not total foreign. 

And it’s market value from TIC, so part of the post-2021 drop is rate-driven price decline, not just selling. 

Par-adjusted would be cleaner but TIC doesn’t make that easy.

And yes — market value, so the 2022 rate spike exaggerates the decline vs. actual quantity sold. 

So, mostly issuance outgrowing a flat official bid, with the 2022 rate shock adding a valuation kicker.

tweets.full_text:
@LanceRoberts Happy to show you that one. Same trend. No changes on conclusions in my view

There is one important difference between your chart and mine.

The data I’m using measures foreign holdings relative to total outstanding Treasury securities, excluding intragovernmental holdings.

What it shows is that the surge in Treasury issuance is no longer being absorbed by foreign buyers. 

That doesn’t necessarily mean foreigners are net sellers as a group — it simply means they are no longer keeping pace with the growth in supply.

It’s foreign official holdings (central banks + SWFs, ~$3.9T), not total foreign. 

And it’s market value from TIC, so part of the post-2021 drop is rate-driven price decline, not just selling. 

Par-adjusted would be cleaner but TIC doesn’t make that easy.

And yes — market value, so the 2022 rate spike exaggerates the decline vs. actual quantity sold. 

So, mostly issuance outgrowing a flat official bid, with the 2022 rate shock adding a valuation kicker.

@LanceRoberts You are welcome. Hope you are doing well. Happy 4th!
